levels.body: A token is often a material object but it can also be something non-physical that is carrying value or meaning. Tokens can be used for indication of facts, feelings and events (“Token, *n*.” Sense 1.a.). Further meanings of tokens include the consideration of quality via an outstanding sign or distinguishing two objects, though they can also be seen as proof of a statement or fact (“Token, *n*.” Sense 3.a.).
worksCited: “Token, N.” *Oxford English Dictionary*, Oxford UP, March 2026, doi.org/10.1093/OED/7009096905. 18 Mar. 2026.

levels.body: “Token” has another symbolic meaning as a portent or omen; an indication of either something good or evil that is to come (“Token, *n*.”, Sense 5.).
worksCited: “Token, N.” *Oxford English Dictionary*, Oxford UP, March 2026, doi.org/10.1093/OED/7009096905. Accessed 18 Mar. 2026.

levels.body: In the context of this poem, “silence and stillness” precedes the word “token” which stresses a juxtaposition. The meaning of token refers to something that is, contrasting stillness and silence, which are defined by what is not. Simultaneously, “the silence was unbroken, and the stillness gave no token” (emphasis added) insinuates that the silence and stillness, usually initiating a comfortable or an uncomfortable pause, are not yet an omen – a sign or warning – for something to come. Despite the insistence that there is no omen, the [[raven]] – described by Poe as “the bird of ill omen” (Poe 165) – already makes itself noticeable prior to the speaker’s conviction by “… rapping, tapping at [the] chamber door” (l.22) and causes the speaker to be faced with darkness and fear (l.24-25). This conceptualizes an ambiguity in which there is indeed a materialization of an ill omen – the raven – which is not yet seen, therefore, only the air of an ill omen encapsulates the speaker without the speaker (wanting) to realize it. Ironically, the speaker claims there is no sign at all, yet interprets the pause as a hopeful moment, shown in his whispering of “Lenore”, indicating that he is merely in denial of a hovering ill-natured portend, which means the speaker has an underlying perception of a token or an omen as something ominously.
worksCited: Poe, Edgar Allan. “The Philosophy of Composition”, *Graham’s Magazine*, vol. XXVIII, no. 4, April 1846, pp. 163-167. Reproduction at: www.eapoe.org/works/essays/philcomp.htm. Accessed 4 July 2024.
